quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 12
quiz.subtitle: Two-Way Tables: Analysis and Probability
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Analyzing categorical data in two-way frequency tables

questions.question:
A shipping company tracks the delivery status of packages sent via Ground or Air.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{On Time} & \text{Late} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Ground} & 425 & 75 & 500 \\ \hline \text{Air} & 480 & 20 & 500 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 905 & 95 & 1{,}000 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the probability that a randomly selected package was sent via Ground, given that it arrived late?
questions.explanation: This is a conditional probability where the condition is that the package was 'Late'. The total number of late packages is 95. Of those 95, 75 were sent via Ground. The probability is $75/95$, which simplifies to $15/19$.

questions.question:
A university analyzed the major of 400 graduating students based on whether they completed an internship.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Internship} & \text{No Internship} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{STEM} & 140 & 60 & 200 \\ \hline \text{Humanities} & 80 & 120 & 200 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 220 & 180 & 400 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a student is selected at random, what is the ratio of Humanities students with no internship to STEM students with no internship?
questions.explanation: Humanities students with no internship = 120. STEM students with no internship = 60. The ratio is 120:60, which simplifies to 2:1.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of a group of 300 employees by their primary mode of transportation and their work schedule.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Morning Shift} & \text{Evening Shift} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Public Transit} & 84 & 36 & 120 \\ \hline \text{Car/Driving} & 126 & 54 & 180 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 210 & 90 & 300 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Based on the table, which of the following statements is true?
options: Morning employees prefer driving over public transit.
Forty percent of evening employees take public transit.
Driving is twice as popular as transit in the evening.
Transit users split evenly between morning and evening.
questions.explanation: Check option B: Total evening employees = 90. Those using transit = 36. $36/90 = 0.4$ (40%). This is correct. Option A is false (126 driving vs 84 transit). Option C is false ($54/36 = 1.5$). Option D is false ($84 \neq 36$).

questions.question:
A boutique sells hats in three colors and two styles. The inventory is listed below.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Wide-Brim} & \text{Fedora} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Tan} & 12 & 18 & 30 \\ \hline \text{Black} & 24 & 6 & 30 \\ \hline \text{Navy} & 10 & 10 & 20 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 46 & 34 & 80 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a Fedora is selected at random, what is the probability that it is NOT Navy?
questions.explanation: The total number of Fedoras is 34. The number of Navy Fedoras is 10. Therefore, the number of Fedoras that are NOT Navy is $34 - 10 = 24$. The probability is $24/34$, which simplifies to $12/17$.

questions.question:
A community center surveyed 500 members about their preferred exercise time.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Weekdays} & \text{Weekends} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Morning} & 180 & 70 & 250 \\ \hline \text{Afternoon} & 100 & 50 & 150 \\ \hline \text{Evening} & 80 & 20 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 360 & 140 & 500 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If an Afternoon exerciser is selected at random, what is the probability they prefer exercising on the weekends?
questions.explanation: The restricted sample space is the Afternoon group (150). The part that prefers Weekends within that group is 50. The probability is $50/150 = 1/3$.

questions.question:
A research study tracked the success of a new training program for employees across two different departments.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Completed Program} & \text{Did Not Complete} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Logistics} & 85 & 15 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Operations} & 60 & 40 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 145 & 55 & 200 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If an employee who did NOT complete the program is selected at random, what is the probability that they work in the Operations department?
questions.explanation: The condition is 'Did Not Complete,' which has a total of 55 employees. Out of these 55, 40 are from the Operations department. The probability is $40/55$, which simplifies to $8/11$.

questions.question:
A grocery store categorized its organic and non-organic fruit sales for one morning.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Organic} & \text{Non-Organic} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Apples} & 42 & 58 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Bananas} & 30 & 70 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Oranges} & 28 & 72 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 100 & 200 & 300 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the ratio of non-organic apples sold to all organic fruit sold?
questions.explanation: Non-organic apples = 58. Total organic fruit = 100. The ratio is $58:100$. Dividing both sides by 2 gives the simplified ratio of $29:50$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of votes for three candidates in a local election by age group.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Candidate X} & \text{Candidate Y} & \text{Candidate Z} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{18–35} & 120 & 150 & 30 & 300 \\ \hline \text{36+} & 180 & 100 & 120 & 400 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 300 & 250 & 150 & 700 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a voter who supported Candidate Z is selected at random, what is the probability that they are in the 18–35 age group?
questions.explanation: The total number of voters for Candidate Z is 150. Among these, 30 are in the 18–35 age group. The probability is $30/150$, which simplifies to $1/5$.

questions.question:
A science museum recorded the types of passes purchased by visitors on a Saturday.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Adult} & \text{Child} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{General Admission} & 115 & 85 & 200 \\ \hline \text{All-Access} & 60 & 40 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 175 & 125 & 300 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Based on the table, which of the following is true?
options: Children are more likely than adults to have All-Access passes.
More than half of all visitors purchased a General Admission child pass.
The probability of a randomly selected child having an All-Access pass is $0.32$.
Exactly $60\%$ of all adults purchased a General Admission pass.
questions.explanation: For option C: Total children = 125. Children with All-Access = 40. $40/125 = 0.32$. This is correct. Option A is false (Child AA% = 32% vs Adult AA% ≈ 34%). Option D is false ($115/175 \approx 65.7\%$).

questions.question:
A travel agency surveyed 400 clients about their preference for mountain or beach vacations.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Mountain} & \text{Beach} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Summer} & 60 & 140 & 200 \\ \hline \text{Winter} & 130 & 70 & 200 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 190 & 210 & 400 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the probability that a client who prefers the beach was surveyed during the winter?
questions.explanation: The restricted sample space is the group of clients who prefer the beach (210). The part within that group surveyed in winter is 70. The probability is $70/210$, which simplifies to $1/3$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently navigate two-way tables, calculate conditional probabilities, interpret marginal vs. joint frequencies, and apply union logic.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on carefully identifying the 'given' condition to select the correct denominator, and remember that 'or' requires subtracting the overlap.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: in two-way tables, the denominator for conditional probability always comes from the row or column total specified in the 'given' clause.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by circling the relevant row or column for each question. Label the total, the target group, and the intersection, then translate the prompt into a fraction.
